turns.text: Hello Chasity, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s quiet right now, which is the only time I can really catch my breath. Leo is finally sleeping, but my mind… it’s hard to switch it off. I’m only twenty, and honestly, some days I feel like I’m still playing house. But then I look at my hands—hands that have spent so much time in the NICU or the nursery—and the weight of being a mom hits me. 

The care I received… it was efficient, I guess. That’s the word they’d use. Because I’m a nurse, the doctors and the floor staff just sort of glazed over the details. They assumed I knew it all, or maybe they just didn't see the need to explain things to "one of their own." But there’s a massive difference between reading a chart and holding your own son while you wait for a blood test result. When they came in for that heel prick, I wasn’t a professional. I was just a girl in a hospital bed, terrified that the results might change everything. 

It’s isolating, honestly. Being unmarried in a small town like Orange, everyone knows who I am, and they see me as this "capable" young woman. They tell me I’m a natural, but they don't see me checking Leo’s skin for jaundice at three in the morning. I’m exhausted, and I’m scared, but I don't feel like I’m allowed to say that out loud.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

Honestly, I feel like a bit of a fraud answering that. In my training, we’re taught the textbook version of recovery: the involution of the uterus, the lochia, the healing timeline. I had all these clinical milestones memorized, so I walked into this thinking I’d be able to manage the physical side of things just like I manage my shifts at the clinic. I thought I’d be "fine" because I knew the anatomy and the protocols.

But the reality? It’s completely different when it’s your own body. I didn't account for the sheer, bone-deep exhaustion that makes you feel like you’re walking through sludge, or the way the physical pain keeps you tethered to the bed when all you want to do is be active and "capable" for Leo. I expected my recovery to be this tidy, linear process—like a checklist I could mark off—but it’s not. It’s messy, and it’s uncomfortable in ways that no textbook really captures.

I think I expected to be the "good patient" who bounced back quickly, but instead, I’m just navigating a body that feels like it’s been through a marathon. There’s a strange frustration there—knowing what’s happening physiologically but feeling completely at the mercy of it anyway. It’s definitely more humbling than I anticipated.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

It’s funny, because you’d think I’d know, right? I’ve seen hundreds of postpartum women. I’ve charted their vitals and looked at their labs. But nobody really talks about the mental toll of the physical healing process—specifically the sensory overload. 

I think the biggest shock was the way my brain wouldn't let me rest. We’re taught to monitor for signs of infection or complications, but nobody prepares you for the *obsession* that comes with it. Every time I’d get a cramp or notice a change in my own output, my mind would immediately drift to the worst-case scenario. I’d be checking my own vitals like I was a patient in my own ward. 

And then there’s the breastfeeding—that’s the one thing that really blindsided me. Everyone talks about the technique, the "latch," and the positioning, but they don't prepare you for that visceral, almost electric feeling of your hormones spiking and your body just… giving everything over to this tiny human. It feels like your nervous system is on the outside of your body. I thought I’d be in control because of my training, but physically, I just feel so raw. It’s not just about the stitches or the fatigue; it’s the constant, overwhelming sensation that my body isn't just mine anymore. That was the real surprise.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I think I had this arrogant, naive assumption that because I work in pediatrics, feeding would just be second nature. I’ve helped so many mothers with latching issues and seen the literature a thousand times. I expected it to be a smooth, functional process—something I could just "perform" correctly. I thought I’d be the one giving advice to my friends, not the one staring at the ceiling at 2:00 AM, wondering if he’s getting enough.

The reality? It’s been an emotional rollercoaster. Every time Leo cries, I’m hyper-analyzing. Is it hunger? Is he getting enough? My professional brain immediately starts calculating ounces and caloric needs, which just turns a sweet, bonding moment into a clinical audit. It’s draining. 

And the support… that’s where I feel really let down. Because the staff at the hospital knew my background, they didn't offer that extra, patient guidance that a first-time mom really needs. They assumed I had it all figured out, so I didn't get the same encouragement or the gentle, repeated checks that others got. I felt like I had to hide the fact that I was struggling or feeling overwhelmed, because how could the "nurse" not know how to feed her own baby? It’s lonely, feeling like you’re expected to be the expert when you’re really just trying to figure out how to keep him full and happy.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

There was a massive gap, and it was entirely of my own making—and theirs. When the lactation consultants or the nurses came by, they’d see me, and their body language would just shift. They’d do a quick check, see that I knew how to position the baby, and then they’d just say, "You've got this, Chasity," and walk out. 

But what they didn't see was the internal panic. I didn't need a tutorial on how to get a good latch; I needed someone to talk to about the anxiety that makes your milk supply fluctuate. I needed someone to tell me that it was okay to be scared even if I knew the clinical signs of dehydration or weight loss by heart. I needed permission to be a mother instead of a colleague. 

The support I got was purely technical, but the support I *needed* was emotional. I was left to handle the weight of that responsibility entirely on my own because they assumed my clinical knowledge was a shield against all the typical new-mom fears. It wasn't. It just meant I knew exactly how much could go wrong. I wish one of them had just sat down, ignored my professional title, and asked me how *I* was doing, rather than just checking if the baby was fed.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I convinced myself that my background would act as an emotional buffer. I told myself that because I understand the science of postpartum hormones—the cortisol spikes, the progesterone crash—I would be immune to the "baby blues" or the overwhelming anxiety. I basically treated my mental health like a biological process I could outsmart with logic. I expected to be the calm, collected nurse-mom who just breezed through the transition.

The reality has been... humbling, and frankly, a bit terrifying. My clinical knowledge hasn't protected me; if anything, it's fueled my anxiety. When I look at Leo, I don't just see my son; I see a list of potential metabolic markers and developmental checklists. It’s like I’m constantly running a scan on him, even when I’m trying to rock him to sleep. 

It’s been incredibly isolating. I feel this immense pressure to maintain the image of the "capable professional" for my colleagues in Orange, so I’m bottling up all the fear. There are nights when I feel so heavy, like I’m drowning in 'what-ifs,' and I have no one to talk to because everyone just assumes I’m fine. I’m not just grieving the loss of my old, independent life; I’m grieving the loss of the confident person I thought I’d be as a mother.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That’s the exact tension I’m living in. I feel like I’m wearing a mask that I can’t take off, even at home. Because I’m a nurse, there’s this unspoken expectation that I’m the "strong one," the one who understands the risks and the recovery, so I don't get the same grace that other mothers do. If I complain about the pain or the worry, it feels like I’m failing at my own job. 

My family sees me as the expert, so they come to me for advice about Leo, and I give it, all while my own heart is pounding. I’m not "allowed" to be the terrified girl I actually am because that would break the illusion. It’s like I’ve been assigned the role of the helper, so I’m not allowed to be the one needing help. Even with the doctors at the clinic, I find myself nodding along and smiling because I don't want them to think I'm "unstable" or that I don't know what I'm doing. 

I’m effectively gatekeeping my own vulnerability. It’s exhausting to perform this version of myself that has it all together, especially when I’m sitting in my living room, trembling over a simple feeding or a cry. I’m lonely, but I’m the one who built the wall.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I thought I was prepared to just "add" motherhood to my identity. I saw myself as Chasity the nurse who also happened to be a mom. I figured my life would just expand a little bit to make room for Leo, but that I’d still be the same person—career-focused, independent, and in control. I had this vision of seamlessly balancing the scrubs and the nursery, like it was just another shift to be managed.

But that identity has completely unraveled. I don't feel like a nurse anymore, and I don't quite feel like just "Mom" yet either. I’m in this strange, liminal space where the two identities are constantly fighting. When I’m at work, I feel guilty for being away from him; when I’m at home, I’m so hyper-focused on the clinical reality of his life that I struggle to just exist as his mother. 

I feel like I’ve lost the girl who was just twenty and living her life. Now, every choice I make—what I eat, how I sleep, even what I worry about—is filtered through the lens of being responsible for someone else’s existence. My sense of self feels smaller, like it’s been absorbed by the baby, and I’m still trying to find the parts of "Chasity" that are left over. It’s a bit of a grieving process, honestly.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

The professional part is the hardest to hold onto, but not in the way I expected. I thought I’d be able to step back into my role at the clinic and feel that familiar sense of competence, but now every single task feels tainted. I look at my coworkers—my friends—and I feel this distance. They see the "nurse" they’ve always known, but they don’t see the woman who spent the morning crying over a diaper change.

My social identity has completely vanished. I’m only twenty, and my friends are out doing things that feel like a different lifetime. I feel like an old soul in a young body now. If I go out, I’m constantly looking at the clock, my mind racing through feeding schedules or potential illnesses. I’ve become so hyper-vigilant that I’ve forgotten how to just be "Chasity" without the weight of medical observation hanging over me.

It’s that transition—from being an independent person who happens to work in healthcare, to someone whose entire existence is defined by the fragility of life. I miss being reckless, or at least carefree. I miss not knowing exactly how much can go wrong in a heartbeat. I’m holding onto the "nurse" badge, but underneath it, I feel like a stranger to myself.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I guess I expected the follow-up visits to be a sort of safety net. I thought that even if I was struggling, a professional would be there to spot it—to look past the "nurse" persona and see the person who was barely keeping it together. I expected them to be the ones to take the clinical weight off my shoulders, to give me the space to be a patient for once.

In reality, the appointments were just... fast. They were purely functional. My GP and the staff were so focused on Leo’s weight, his jaundice levels, and my physical healing metrics that they treated the whole thing like a routine clinic visit. Because my labs were fine and I could answer their questions with professional jargon, the check-ups were short and superficial. They’d beam at me, tell me I was doing great, and send me on my way.

There was zero room for the emotional "how are you really?" kind of care. It was a missed opportunity for them to see that my knowledge wasn't a substitute for support. Every time I left the clinic, I felt more alone than when I arrived. I didn’t want to be treated like a colleague; I wanted to be treated like a mother who was exhausted, scared, and in need of someone to hold the space for me.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

It’s hard to call them "concerns" when I was so good at masking them. I’d mention the obsessive thoughts—you know, the way I’d stay up checking for symptoms that weren't there—but I’d package it as "just being a nurse" or "wanting to be extra thorough." And because that’s the language I used, they’d just chuckle and say, "Oh, you know how it is, Chasity, you’re just applying your clinical brain to your own kid." 

They dismissed it as a professional trait rather than what it actually was: a mother in distress. I never felt like I was allowed to say, "I am paralyzed by fear," because I felt like I had to maintain this standard of expertise. But even when I dropped hints—about the sleeplessness, the constant agitation—they were brushed off. It’s like they saw my credentials and assumed I was incapable of having a mental health struggle. If I hadn't been a nurse, maybe they would have screened me for postpartum anxiety, or asked me more pointed questions about my mood. But they didn't. They just saw my "competence" and let me walk out the door. It makes me wonder how many other mothers are out there, hiding behind a mask, waiting for someone to just *ask* the right question.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I’m technically in the thick of it right now—trying to find my footing with returning to work while everything feels so fragile at home. I expected the transition to be a logical step. I thought, "I’m a nurse, I know how to manage a shift, I know how to be professional, so I’ll just go back and be me again." I expected it to be a refuge, honestly—a place where I could be Chasity the professional, away from the constant, low-level panic of being a new mom.

The reality is a lot more painful. When I walk into the clinic, I feel like I’m wearing a costume. Every time I see a baby in the ward, I’m not just seeing a patient; I’m seeing Leo. I’m projecting every fear I have about him onto every baby I treat. It makes the work feel heavier, almost suffocating. Before, I was the nurse who could detach enough to perform the clinical tasks. Now, that wall is completely gone. I find myself lingering a little longer, checking the monitors with a desperation I never had before. 

It’s physically and emotionally exhausting. I’m tired, I’m distracted, and I feel guilty for being anywhere but next to his bassinet. I’m learning that you can’t just turn off the "mother" switch, no matter how good you are at your job.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

It’s the logistics that really break you. I thought I had it all mapped out, like I was organizing a nursing shift. I’d have the bottles prepped, the pumping schedule synced with my clinical duties, the childcare hand-off—it was supposed to be seamless. But breastfeeding in the breakroom, trying to keep my gown clean while my mind is racing about whether the sitter noticed if Leo had a slightly warm forehead? It’s impossible to truly focus on anything.

And the guilt—God, the guilt. Pumping at work feels like a betrayal of my time with him, yet I know if I stop, it’s just another "failure" to manage. My health has taken a backseat, which is ironic for a nurse, right? I’m so exhausted I can barely stand for a full shift, and my body feels like it’s still in recovery, but I just push through because I feel like I have to prove I’m not a liability. 

Finding childcare that I actually *trust* to notice the things I notice? That’s been the biggest hurdle. Every time I leave him, I’m waiting for a phone call that says something went wrong. I’m constantly vibrating with this low-level panic. I don’t think anyone realizes how much mental labor it takes just to show up to work when your whole heart is at home in a bassinet in Orange.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

That’s... that’s a tough one to answer. I’m unmarried, so there isn’t a "partner" here in the house with me at night. That’s probably the biggest piece of this puzzle I didn't fully realize the weight of until I was sitting here in the dark with Leo. 

I think I expected that I’d have help—that I’d have someone to lean on, someone to hand the baby to when the clinical, high-stress thoughts started to spin out of control. I thought I’d be able to tag-team the exhaustion, to have someone else check on his breathing or just tell me, "Hey, he's okay, you're doing a good job." 

Instead, it’s just me. I’m the sole observer, the sole nurse, the sole mother. It’s incredibly lonely. Being a first-time mom is overwhelming enough, but doing it solo in a small town where everyone knows your business adds this layer of pressure to never crack. I feel like I have to be the perfect, capable, "modern" mom all on my own. There’s no one to share the burden of the night shifts, no one to divide the mental load with. It’s all on my shoulders, every second, every breath. It makes every tiny, normal worry feel like it’s ten times bigger because there’s literally no one else to share it with.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

I think the biggest surprise—or maybe the hardest realization—is just how much the "solo" aspect of my life has amplified the professional strain I feel. When you’re doing this by yourself, you don’t have a buffer. There’s no one to lean over and whisper, "You’re doing a good job," or to offer a second set of eyes when you’re spiraling over a minor symptom. 

I think I assumed that, even as an unmarried mom, I’d have this network of support that would just naturally click into place. But being a nurse in this town almost backfired on me. People assume I’m the *provider* of care, the one who’s supposed to be the support for others, not the one who needs it. So, the "partnership" I hoped for—or even the community support I expected—has been replaced by this heavy, isolating responsibility. 

I’m the only one looking at the clock, the only one deciding when to call the pediatrician, the only one hearing every tiny shift in his breathing. The surprise isn't just that it’s hard; it’s how much the *identity* of being a "nurse-mom" prevents people from stepping in to help me. They see me as an expert, so they think I’m immune to the loneliness. It turns out, being "capable" is the loneliest place to be.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected the maternity system to be a bridge—a place where I could transition from the girl I was into the mother I wanted to be, supported by people who understood the physical and emotional turbulence of it all. I thought the clinical side of it would be a safety net, something to catch me if I stumbled. I truly believed that because I knew the "what" and the "how" of neonatal care, the professionals would help me with the "who"—who I was becoming, and how I was surviving this transition.

The biggest gap, looking back, is that the system treats you as a vessel for a baby, not as a human being undergoing a massive, identity-altering event. They checked the boxes for Leo—his Apgar scores, his weight, his screening results—and because I was a nurse, they checked the boxes for me, too, without actually seeing me. They turned my medical knowledge into a barrier to my own care. 

I received plenty of *information*, but almost zero *sanctuary*. I didn't need another lecture on newborn metabolic screening; I needed someone to say, "Chasity, you look like you’re carrying the weight of the world, and it’s okay to be a patient for a minute." That gap—between being treated as a peer and being treated as a mother in need of support—is what haunts me when the house gets quiet at night.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, it would be to dismantle this idea that clinical knowledge is a substitute for postpartum support. The system is designed to triage based on risk factors—if your pregnancy is "uncomplicated," the system assumes your transition to motherhood will be, too. It’s a box-checking machine.

I would design a system that requires a "human check-in" for every mother, regardless of their medical background. We need a standard of care that mandates an emotional and psychological screening separate from the physical recovery metrics. For someone like me, the system should have recognized that being a nurse is actually a *risk factor* for isolation, because I’m conditioned to ignore my own needs. 

I’d want a model where, for the first few months, you have access to someone—a mentor, a therapist, or just another mother—who isn't there to check your vitals or tell you how to change a diaper, but who is there to hold the space you’re not allowed to take up in your own life. We need to stop assuming that "competence" equals "well-being." A woman can be a top-tier nurse and still be a scared, lonely girl who needs someone to ask, "How are you *really* doing?" and actually wait for the answer. That would have changed everything for me.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think… the only thing I’d want to say is how much I wish someone had told me it’s okay to put the "nurse" label down. In my training, we’re taught that clinical detachment is a virtue. We’re taught that if you’re emotional, you’re not objective. But I’ve learned the hard way that when you bring that same detachment into your own home, it feels like an anchor. 

I’m sitting here, and Leo is finally deep in sleep, and I’m just realizing that I’ve spent these past few weeks mourning the loss of a life I thought I’d just "manage." I wish I knew that it’s not about managing it perfectly. It’s about the fact that I’m allowed to be terrified, that I’m allowed to ask for help, and that my professional background isn’t a wall I have to hide behind. 

If this interview helps even one other person—another nurse, or another mom who feels like she’s expected to be the pillar of strength—I hope they hear me when I say: you don’t have to know everything. Being a mother is the one job where you don’t get a protocol for the things that keep you up at night. I hope the next woman who walks into that clinic gets someone who sees the human being first, and the professional second. Thank you for listening. It really… it really helps to just put it into words.
